metadata.projectName: Western Narrative World — Lonesome Dove Tradition Character Epic

objectives.description: Create a character-driven Western narrative world in the Lonesome Dove tradition — sprawling ensemble, elegiac tone, literary ambition, grounded in the harsh realities of the Old West
objectives.kpi: Narrative world complete with characters, setting, plot architecture, and thematic framework

objectives.description: Build a community-in-crisis narrative centered on a specific place and time (1880s) where the converging pressures of railroad expansion, land-title chaos, racial violence, and the end of the open range create dramatic necessity
objectives.kpi: Setting fully specified with historical anchors, location descriptions, and narrative function

objectives.description: Populate the world with a diverse ensemble whose perspectives represent the multi-ethnic, multi-gendered historical reality of the West — including voices systematically marginalized in the traditional Western canon
objectives.kpi: 15+ characters with full backstories, conflicts, voice traits, and LD parallels

objectives.description: Achieve Lonesome Dove's tonal range — humor, tenderness, unadorned violence, elegiac reflection, philosophical rumination — without imitation of LD's surface furniture
objectives.kpi: All 6 tonal registers present in chapter grid and character voice design

objectives.description: Structure the narrative to sustain tension across a doorstop-scale work, avoiding middle-act sag, cast bloat, and the prestige-TV ambiguous ending trap
objectives.kpi: Each part has distinct dramatic identity; every 5 chapters provide escalation or revelation

objectives.description: Position the narrative to reach the 'bridge reader' — the audience that wants both literary depth and narrative momentum, both genre authenticity and cultural relevance
objectives.kpi: Prose that signals literary quality from page one; characters complex enough for book club debate

concepts.name: Moxley Town Chronicle Structure
concepts.description: Fixed-point narrative centered on Moxley, Texas (Oct 1882–Apr 1883). Multiple-perspective 'town chronicle' where each character's private drama accumulates into a collective portrait of a community at the moment of its transformation by the railroad. Structural model: Winesburg, Ohio filtered through frontier violence.

evaluation.rationale: Strong inversion of LD's journey model. The town-as-crucible forces character collision rather than sequential revelation. Risk: requires expert pacing to avoid feeling static.

concepts.name: The Bitterroot Trail Journey Structure
concepts.description: Freighting expedition (12 wagons, 60 mules, 37 people) carrying secret mining equipment from San Antonio to Montana Territory (April–October 1874). The expedition is the crucible inside which every character is tested. 96 chapters, 350k words, 4 parts, 5 rotating POVs.

evaluation.rationale: Journey-as-crucible is LD's proven structure. The mining conspiracy cargo elevates it beyond cattle-drive imitation. Risk: middle-act sag across 30-chapter Part II.

concepts.name: Eulalia Quince — The Widow Who Outlasted the Country
concepts.description: 58-year-old widow who has held her Brazos claim alone for 40 years. Clara Allen without family — pure stubborn endurance. Her will vs. the recognition that endurance was never the same as living.

concepts.name: Moses 'Mose' Whitfield — Buffalo Soldier Turned Tracker
concepts.description: Born enslaved, emancipated at 16, enlisted in 10th Cavalry at 18. Served 14 years on the southern plains. Mustered out in 1880 to find no place for a Black man with a cavalry pension and a vocabulary that makes white settlers uneasy. A Deets who knows the cage.

concepts.name: Linh Tam — Railroad Widow with a Repeating Rifle
concepts.description: Chinese widow crossing a thousand miles of hostile country alone (1882, the year of the Chinese Exclusion Act). Searching for her husband's murdered cousin. Carries a Henry rifle. Listens more than she speaks and understands more English than she lets on.

concepts.name: Wanbli Tokahe — The Comanche Who Learned White Laws
concepts.description: Nokoni Comanche, captured at 12, sent to Carlisle Indian School, renamed 'Thomas Logan.' Returns west as government interpreter, trusted by neither whites nor his own people. Speaks four languages and is fluent in despair. No direct LD parallel — he is what the novel could not see.

concepts.name: Ismael 'Izzy' Pacheco — Tejano Who Rode with Quantrill
concepts.description: Lost his family's Rio Grande land to gringo courts, fell in with Quantrill's raiders at 16, rode through Lawrence in 1863. Has spent 20 years trying to outrun what he saw. Works as a horse breaker — the only trade that asks nothing of his tongue.

concepts.name: Lydia 'Liddy' Strange — The Whore with a Business Plan
concepts.description: Bought her freedom at 26, owns the only saloon in Moxley plus half the lots on main street. Unofficial mayor, banker, and confessor. The railroad is coming and a Chicago speculator is buying everything around her.

concepts.name: Thaddeus Grimes — The Blacksmith Who Believes in Civilization
concepts.description: Born free in Boston, founded Moxley's first smithy. Town's moral center for 20 years — judges disputes, holds debts, reads the Bible in Greek. His faith in progress is being tested by the speculator who wants 'his kind' gone.

concepts.name: Senora Antonia Covarrubias — The Hacendada Without an Hacienda
concepts.description: Born to a great land-grant family of New Mexico, watched 300,000 acres dissolve through American courts, bad marriages, and taxes. Now lives in a four-room adobe with 9 cats, writing a family history no one will publish. Remembers the West before the Americans came.

concepts.name: Dual Male Protagonist Engine
concepts.description: Two co-equal male protagonists with opposing philosophies bound by shared history — the Call/Gus dynamic identified by the Evaluator as the most efficient structural engine for plot, theme, and emotional weight.

concepts.name: The 1880s Elegy Window
concepts.description: Setting the narrative in the 1880s (specifically 1882–1883) to maximize the elegiac register — barbed wire going up, Indian Wars concluding, buffalo near extinction, open range in its final decade.

research.question: What was the daily life of a Chinese woman in a railroad camp actually like? Legal status, work, treatment?
How were Spanish and Mexican land grants adjudicated in Texas after 1848? What court cases set precedent?
What was the physical layout, social structure, and daily operation of the Carlisle Indian School in the 1870s?
What was the process of filing a mining claim in Texas in the 1880s? Barriers for non-white claimants?
1874 freighting routes, wagon types, mule team logistics, daily mileage, supply requirements for a 5-month crossing
Lakota culture, social structure, spiritual practices in 1874; Treaty of Fort Laramie (1868) and its collapse; 1874 Black Hills gold discovery aftermath
Reconstruction-era racial dynamics, Black teamsters in the West, freedom suits, legal status of formerly enslaved people in Louisiana 1865–1874
Medical practices of the 1870s, frontier medicine, Civil War nursing, pharmaceutical availability
What was the route of the Fort Worth and Denver City Railway through Texas in the 1880s? Which towns were bypassed and which boomed?
What was the ecological state of the Llano Estacado in the 1880s — grass species, animal populations, water sources, seasonal weather?

assumptions.assumption: A large ensemble (10–15 significant characters) is sustainable in a single literary novel without fragmenting reader attention
assumptions.ifFalse: Narrative becomes unfollowable; reader abandons around page 200
assumptions.validationMethod: Write 50-page test with 5 POVs; beta readers must describe all 5 characters and relationships after 50 pages

assumptions.assumption: The Western genre supports a viable literary-fiction audience in 2026–27
assumptions.ifFalse: Strong reviews, middling sales; novel does not earn out advance
assumptions.validationMethod: Analyze sales trajectories of comparable literary Westerns (The Son, News of the World, Whiskey When We're Dry)

assumptions.assumption: A non-Native author can write Native characters with sufficient depth and authenticity to avoid causing harm
assumptions.ifFalse: Either erasure or harmful representation; Native reviewers condemn the work
assumptions.validationMethod: Engage Native readers and historical consultant at outline stage; do not write Native POV without Native editorial input

assumptions.assumption: The tonal ambition of LD (humor, tenderness, violence, elegy) is achievable in a single draft cycle
assumptions.ifFalse: Novel feels tonally uneven — humor undercuts drama, violence feels gratuitous
assumptions.validationMethod: Write complete 60–80 page act at full tonal range; test on readers for jarring shifts

assumptions.assumption: The market differentiation between 'literary Western' and 'Western genre novel' is clear enough for positioning and review placement
assumptions.ifFalse: Novel falls between categories — genre readers don't find it, literary readers don't pick it up
assumptions.validationMethod: Test positioning language with readers: 'novel of the American West' vs. 'literary fiction set in the 1870s' vs. 'epic Western'

decisions.decision: Set the narrative in the 1880s (1882–1883 or 1874) — the open range's last decade, maximizing elegiac weight
decisions.rationale: The elegy is the genre's most powerful note and requires historical specificity. Barbed wire, railroad expansion, Indian Wars concluding, buffalo extinction create a world where characters know they're late.

decisions.decision: Protagonist should be a rancher/drover/horse trader/freighter, not a gunslinger or lawman — story about making a living, not making a name
decisions.rationale: LD's best work is about labor. Texture, stakes, and built-in clock. A gunslinger's story is about death; a rancher's is about life, which makes death matter more.

decisions.decision: Include at least one co-equal woman's POV with her own arc, stakes, and climax — not a subplot but a parallel narrative thread
decisions.rationale: The single greatest weakness of the Western genre is the marginalization of women. Lonesome Dove partially corrects this (Clara, Lorena). Historically honest and narratively strengthening.

decisions.decision: Let the antagonist have a legitimate grievance — a worldview the reader can't entirely dismiss even while fearing them
decisions.rationale: The Judge works because philosophically compelling. Blue Duck works because hatred is earned. A villain who is just bad is dramatically inert.

decisions.decision: Kill at least one primary character before the final act — death should feel wasteful, random, and unbearably sad
decisions.rationale: LD kills Sean and Jake before the climax. Establishes world doesn't care about narrative justice. Reader is braced for later losses. The randomness IS the theme.

decisions.decision: Structure around two co-equal male protagonists with opposing philosophies bound by shared history
decisions.rationale: The Call/Gus dynamic generates plot, theme, and emotional weight more efficiently than any other structural choice. Love that cannot be spoken, expressed through action.
alternatives: Larger ensemble with no dual anchor

decisions.decision: Town chronicle OR journey narrative: two viable structural models emerged. Recommendation: begin with the town chronicle for originality and tighter historical window, but preserve the journey as a possible Part II–III engine or for a second novel
decisions.rationale: The town chronicle (Moxley, 1882–83) is structurally more original as an LD inversion. The journey narrative (Bitterroot Trail, 1874) is proven but risks LD imitation. Both are fully specified and either is viable.
alternatives: Hybrid (town Part I, journey Parts II–IV)

deliverables.name: Complete Character Dossiers (15 characters)
deliverables.spec: Full physical description, voice/dialect sample, backstory before chapter 1, core want vs. core need, fear inventory, skill set, death note for doomed characters. Each dossier 2-3 pages.
acceptanceCriteria: Each character has at least two contradictory traits
Each character's voice is distinguishable from all others
Each character passes the 'scenes without white people present' test where applicable

deliverables.name: Geography and Routes Map
deliverables.spec: Full map of Moxley and surrounding territory OR the 1,800-mile San Antonio-to-Montana route with all waypoints, water sources, and dangers. Llano Estacado geography verified against historical sources. Seasonal weather pattern overlay.
acceptanceCriteria: Every location mentioned in the chapter grid appears on the map
Water sources and distances are historically accurate
Terrain shapes at least 3 significant plot events

deliverables.name: Timeline and Event Log
deliverables.spec: Day-by-day timeline for the narrative's timespan. Character location at every chapter. Historical events happening concurrently. Distance traveled per day (where applicable).
acceptanceCriteria: No character is in two places at once
Weather/season conditions are consistent across chapters
Historical events are correctly dated

deliverables.name: Dialect and Dialogue Guide
deliverables.spec: Period-appropriate vocabulary by region, education markers by character, profanity policy, racial address conventions of the period. Verified against 1870s–1880s primary sources.
acceptanceCriteria: No anachronistic words or phrases
Every POV character has a distinct dialogue fingerprint
Dialogue read aloud passes the 'not cringing' test

deliverables.name: Part I Complete Draft (14 chapters, ~48,000 words)
deliverables.spec: Full first draft of Part I establishing all POV characters, the central container (town or expedition), the cargo mystery, and the ensemble dynamics. Ends with the Guadalupe crossing (journey model) or the railroad's arrival (town model).
acceptanceCriteria: All primary POVs established in fixed rotation
Reader can name every significant character after 100 pages
At least one scene of unguarded tenderness between non-romantic characters
Closing chapter leaves reader with specific ache of something beginning to end

deliverables.name: Full Narrative Draft (250,000 words)
deliverables.spec: Complete draft at compressed length — all four parts, all character arcs, all thematic throughlines. Set pieces compressed but functional.
acceptanceCriteria: All character arcs traceable from introduction to resolution
Middle act passes 'every 5 chapters something changes' test
Ending is emotionally complete without sentimental resolution

deliverables.name: Final Manuscript (320,000–380,000 words)
deliverables.spec: Expanded final draft with all set pieces fully written, all thematic throughlines paid off, all character voices refined. Ready for editorial review.
acceptanceCriteria: Passes sensitivity read by relevant community consultants
Beta readers report finishing within 3 weeks (grip test)
At least 60% of early readers say 'would enthusiastically recommend'

dependencies.name: Geography/routes map must be complete before any travel/location chapters drafted
Character dossiers (especially voice samples) must be complete before ensemble chapters
dependencies.resolution: Complete DEL-1 for all primary characters before ensemble interaction scenes

dependencies.name: Native representation must be reviewed by Indigenous consultant at outline stage
dependencies.resolution: Engage Native reader before drafting Native POV material

dependencies.name: The cargo/railroad mystery must be planted in chapters 4, 12, 13 before the reveal
dependencies.resolution: Each plant hint must be visible on re-read but not obvious on first pass

dependencies.name: Isaiah's daughter's death only lands if Chapter 5 establishes the freedom suit clearly
dependencies.resolution: Chapter 5 must establish specific stakes: daughter's name, enslaver's location, years of saving

risks.description: The Yellowstone Problem — characters carry 21st-century sensibilities in period dress. A woman in 1875 who talks like a 2026 feminist without suffering any cost.
risks.mitigation: For every progressive choice, identify the specific price the character would pay in their time and place. Anachronistic values without consequences = fan service.
risks.trigger: A character says something that reads like a 2026 op-ed

risks.description: The Noble Savage / Vanishing Indian trope — Native characters as mystical wisdom-dispensers with no interior complexity or agendas beyond helping/threatening white protagonists.
risks.mitigation: Native characters must have their own storylines, conflicts within their communities, and scenes with each other unrelated to white people.
risks.trigger: A Native character speaks mostly in metaphor or appears only to help/hinder white characters

risks.description: The Magnificent Seven Flattening — ensemble of 10–15 characters where each embodies exactly one defining trait and never deepens.
risks.mitigation: Every ensemble member needs at least two traits that conflict. 'One note plus one contradictory note' is the minimum viable secondary character.
risks.trigger: Any secondary character can be described entirely in three words

risks.description: The Woman Problem — every female character exists only as motivation for men. Dead wife, kidnapped love interest, prostitute with heart of gold.
risks.mitigation: Before writing any female character, answer: what does she want that has nothing to do with any man? What does she fear that isn't about being hurt by a man? Who is she when no men are watching?
risks.trigger: A female character's primary scene function is to be rescued, mourned, desired, or advised by a male character

risks.description: Middle-Act Sag — journey/town narrative loses structural tension in the middle third. Chapters become 'and then they rode some more / and then another day passed in town.'
risks.mitigation: Every 5th chapter must provide escalation: new landscape, threat, revelation, or character transformation. The middle must be the most eventful section, not the least.
risks.trigger: 30+ pages since last meaningful event; beta reader says 'nothing happens in the middle'

risks.description: The Nostalgia/Reckoning Trapdoor — the Western genre's unresolved tension between elegy (mourning a lost world) and indictment (condemning genocide). Attempting both without clear authorial stance produces ethical confusion.
risks.mitigation: Before drafting, decide privately: is this a reckoning wearing elegy's clothes, or elegy interrupted by reckoning? Characters can disagree with the narrative stance; the narrative must know what it thinks.
risks.trigger: Reader can't tell if they're supposed to cheer or grieve a scene

risks.description: Cast Bloat — 15+ named 'main' characters introduced in first 100 pages. Reader cannot track who is who.
risks.mitigation: Stagger introductions. No more than 3–4 significant named characters per chapter for first 200 pages. Only 8 expedition/town members get interiority or significant dialogue.
risks.trigger: Reader has to flip back to figure out who a character is

risks.description: The Lonesome Dove Imitation — borrows surface furniture (cattle drive, retired Rangers, saloon) without LD's specific magic.
risks.mitigation: Conduct structural analysis of what LD is actually doing, then do something structurally different with those tools. If reader says 'this feels like Lonesome Dove' as compliment, you've failed.
risks.trigger: A character says 'I'm too old for this' in chapter 1

risks.description: Genre stigma — 'I don't read Westerns' barrier prevents literary audience from discovering the book
risks.mitigation: Front-load humanity, not genre. First page must signal interiority, not setting. Cover design must NOT look like a traditional Western.
risks.trigger: Book positioned as 'a Western' rather than 'a novel of the American West' in marketing

constraints.description: Cannot present displacement of Native peoples as neutral progress or Manifest Destiny as benign
Cannot depict sexual assault as narrative device for female character development
Cannot present Confederate Lost Cause mythology uncritically
Cannot erase non-white presence from the historical West — 'all-white frontier town' reads as willful erasure
Cannot do pure white-hat/black-hat moral simplicity — literary readership rejects it
Length: 600+ pages demands extraordinary justification in current publishing market
The 'Lonesome Dove comparison' is inevitable and usually unflattering — the novel must be strong enough to survive it
Traditional Western reader market is demographically narrow and shrinking — cannot rely on core Western audience for commercial viability

successCriteria.metric: Every primary character has at least one scene acting against expectation that forces reader re-evaluation
successCriteria.rationale: The moment of contradiction IS the character — what separates literary characters from archetypes

successCriteria.metric: Landscape directly causes or prevents at least 3 significant plot events
successCriteria.rationale: If the plot is portable, the Western is cosmetic. The land must be a co-author.

successCriteria.metric: At least one scene of unguarded tenderness between non-romantically-linked characters
successCriteria.rationale: Makes violence hurt; proves author understands love not just violence

successCriteria.metric: Closing 10% must contain emotional loss NOT redeemed by ending — lower key than beginning
successCriteria.threshold: Final chapter must not feel like victory even if characters survive

successCriteria.metric: At least 3 women with distinct internal lives independent of male characters
successCriteria.rationale: Western canon weakest here; exceeding bar earns place

successCriteria.metric: Protagonist fails someone they love through act of commission or omission — failure never fully forgiven
successCriteria.threshold: One irreparable moral failure with persistent consequences
successCriteria.rationale: Protagonist must be damaged enough that redemption is impossible; reader closes book troubled not comforted

successCriteria.threshold: 25+ independent bookstores as staff pick AND 5+ book club guide downloads within 3 months
successCriteria.rationale: Book clubs are the engine of literary fiction sales

specifications.title: Narrative World Specification — Moxley Town Chronicle Model
specifications.content: Fixed-point narrative centered on Moxley, Texas (October 1882–April 1883). The railroad — and the modern world it represents — arrives to either save or destroy the town. Multi-perspective 'town chronicle' in the tradition of Winesburg, Ohio, filtered through frontier violence. 15 characters' private dramas accumulate into a collective portrait of a community at the moment of its transformation. The townsite sits on contested land — Spanish grant overlay on Comanche hunting grounds overlay on Anglo squatters' claims — making the legal battle over its future a physical map of the West's foundational dispossessions.

specifications.title: Narrative World Specification — The Bitterroot Trail Journey Model
specifications.content: Freighting expedition (12 wagons, 60 mules, 37 people) carrying secret mining equipment from San Antonio, Texas to Montana Territory (April–October 1874). 96 chapters, ~350,000 words, 4 parts. Five rotating POV characters: Emmett Cole (drifting ex-Ranger turned wagon master), Ruth Tolliver (Union Army nurse who killed her abusive husband and is fleeing the consequences), Isaiah Brown (formerly enslaved Black teamster saving to free his daughter), Caleb Cross (frightened young man whose violence escalates across three kills), and Two Bears/Mato Najin (Lakota war chief watching the buffalo disappear and the whites approach). The cargo is illegal mining equipment destined for treaty-protected Lakota land. The expedition is the crucible inside which every character is tested. Part I: The Gathering (14 chapters), Part II: The Crossing (30 chapters), Part III: The High Plains (28 chapters), Part IV: The Reckoning (24 chapters).

specifications.title: Genre and Tonal Specification
specifications.content: Lonesome Dove tonal palette: rough comedy, unadorned violence (fast, confusing, ugly), elegiac reflection, tender domesticity, philosophical rumination, atmospheric dread. The elegy is the dominant key — every other register modulates toward or away from it. Structure follows LD's four-beat cycle: comic domesticity → rising threat → violent payoff → elegiac aftermath. Violence must be brief, brutal, and consequential — never choreographed for excitement. Humor must emerge from character, not jokes inserted for levity. Prose must favor precision over floridity — heat is a pressure, distance is a measurement, water is a quantity. 'Poetic, yes — florid, no.'

specifications.title: Representation and Sensitivity Specification
specifications.content: The post-Civil War West was profoundly diverse: approximately 1 in 4 cowboys was Black, Chinese railroad workers and communities existed across the West, Mexican/Tejano communities predated Anglo settlement by generations, and Indigenous nations had sophisticated political structures. A 'realistic' Western in 2026 must reflect this diversity or have a narrative reason for not doing so. Native characters must have their own storylines with conflicts within their own communities. Female characters must have agency, interiority, and arcs that aren't about men. Chinese characters must not be rendered in broken-English dialect. Black characters must have range beyond processing racial trauma. Before publishing: engage Native readers from the specific nations depicted, a Western history academic, a sensitivity reader with 19th-century racial dynamics expertise, and a historian of Chinese American experience in the West.

specifications.title: Publishing and Audience Specification
specifications.content: Target audience: the 'bridge reader' who wants both literary depth and narrative momentum, both genre authenticity and cultural relevance. Primary market: literary fiction readers who need permission to enjoy a Western. Secondary: book club core (women 35–65), prestige TV audience, historical fiction devotees. Tertiary: Western traditionalists, genre expanders, academic readers. Positioning: 'a novel of the American West' or 'literary fiction set in the 1870s/1880s' — NOT 'a Western.' Cover design must avoid cowboy silhouettes, sunset vistas, and revolver imagery. The Lonesome Dove comparison should be earned by reviewers, not courted by marketing. Adaptation viability: HIGH. Ideal format: 6–10 episode prestige limited series (HBO, FX, Amazon, Apple). Estimated draft time: 18 months across three drafts.
